introduction: At its core, a shell script is a simple text file containing a list of commands that the shell parses and executes sequentially. Instead of typing the same commands manually every day, DevOps engineers write Bash scripts to automate complex software builds, trigger daily database backups, clean up server disk sectors, and launch local development clusters.

Bash (Bourne Again SHell) scripting is the absolute baseline programming language of infrastructure automation. When a virtual machine boots in AWS, or a container initializes inside Kubernetes, it runs boot configuration scripts written in Bash. Understanding how to use variables, loops, conditional statements, and error codes is what allows you to replace hours of manual administration work with a single automated script.

This module starts from the absolute beginning: configuring your script files with the shebang syntax header, declaring and using local variables, performing numerical calculations, constructing logical checks with if-else conditionals, using loop statements to process files lists, and writing helper functions with positional parameters.
what_you_will_learn: The role of the shebang header line and files executable flags.
Declaring shell variables and referencing scopes values.
Performing numerical math calculations in the shell.
Constructing logical checks and if-else branches.
Writing loops (for and while) to process variables sets.
Encapsulating logic blocks inside custom shell functions.
overview.prerequisites: Completion of Linux File System & Permissions course.
learning_outcomes: Build fully automated executable scripting utilities from scratch.
Incorporate variable values, conditions, and computations.
Write loops that scan directories lists and perform operations.
Audit exit status codes to check success flags.

theory.title: The Magic of the Shebang: How Scripts Boot
theory.definition: The special combination of characters #!/bin/bash at the very beginning of a file that tells your computer exactly which program should run the script.
theory.explanation:
Imagine you write a letter, but you write it in French. If you hand it to an English speaker, they'll stare at it blankly. You need to write a little note at the top saying: 'Translator, please read this!'

In the world of coding, files containing shell commands are just plain text. When you run a script, the Linux kernel looks at the very first line of the file. If it sees the characters #! (called the 'Shebang' or 'Hash-bang'), it knows it needs to hand the file over to an interpreter.

By writing #!/bin/bash at the very top, you are saying: 'Hey Linux, feed all the text in this file directly into the Bash interpreter so it can execute them!' Without this, Linux might use a different, more basic shell, and your advanced script might crash.
theory.why_exists: Ensures your script executes reliably on any Linux machine, regardless of which shell the current user is active in.
theory.real_world_example: Writing #!/bin/bash on line 1 of your backup script, followed by echo 'Starting backup...' on line 2.
theory.remember: The shebang must be on the absolute first line of your script file. Do not put spaces before the # or after the !.
theory.pro_tip: You can locate the path of your active bash binary by running the command: which bash.
theory.common_mistakes: Writing comments or blank lines before the shebang. The shebang MUST be on line 1, character 1, or Linux will completely ignore it!
theory.best_practices: Always write #!/bin/bash instead of plain #!/bin/sh to ensure access to modern Bash features like arrays and advanced conditions.

commands.name: chmod +x
commands.purpose: Grants executable permissions to a script file so it can be run directly.
commands.parameters: filename.sh: The script file to make executable.
commands.output_explanation: Marks backup.sh as runnable, enabling execution using standard ./ path notation.
commands.when_to_use: Run this once immediately after creating a new script file.

commands.name: ./filename.sh
commands.purpose: Executes a script file in the current folder path.
commands.parameters: filename.sh: The name of the script file.
commands.expected_output: (Runs the commands stored inside your backup.sh script)
commands.output_explanation: The shell loads backup.sh, reads shebang interpreter, and executes commands line by line.
commands.when_to_use: Use this whenever you need to run your custom automation scripts.

theory.summary: The shebang (#!/bin/bash) on line 1 tells Linux to interpret the file using Bash. Run chmod +x and use ./ to execute the script.

theory.title: Variables & The Golden Rule of No Spaces
theory.definition: Named boxes in memory where you can store text or numbers to reuse them throughout your script.
theory.explanation:
Variables in Bash are like labeled drawers. You put some text inside the drawer, write a label on it, and slide it shut. Later, you can pull the drawer open by using its label!

Here is how you declare a variable in Bash:
SERVER_NAME="production-db"

To retrieve and print the variable, prefix it with a dollar sign ($):
echo "Connecting to $SERVER_NAME..."

| Operator / Syntax | Description | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| = | Declares variable (no spaces allowed) | NAME="John" |
| $ | References value of a variable | echo $NAME |
theory.why_exists: Allows your scripts to be dynamic and reusable, so you can change a single variable at the top instead of editing fifty lines of code.
theory.real_world_example: Setting DB_BACKUP_PATH="/var/backups/db" and then copying database dumps to $DB_BACKUP_PATH.
theory.remember: You MUST NOT put spaces around the equal sign (=) when assigning variables! If you write SERVER_NAME = "production-db" (with spaces), Bash will get confused and try to run SERVER_NAME as a command!
theory.pro_tip: You can wrap your variable names in curly braces ${VARIABLE_NAME} to safely isolate them from surrounding text (e.g. echo "${NAME}_backup").
theory.common_mistakes: Adding spaces around the = sign when declaring, or forgetting the $ when referencing the variable value.
theory.best_practices: Use uppercase letters for environment and global variables, and lowercase for temporary local variables.

commands.name: read
commands.purpose: Prompts the user for input keyboard values and saves it into a variable.
commands.parameters: -p prompt_msg: Displays a message before waiting for input. variable_name: The target storage variable.
commands.expected_output: Enter server username: (waits for keyboard typing)
commands.output_explanation: Prompts you to type a value, then saves whatever you type into $input_user.
commands.when_to_use: Use this when you want to build interactive scripts that require user input at runtime.

theory.summary: Bash variables are defined using NAME="value" (strictly no spaces around =). Access them using $NAME or ${NAME}. Use read to prompt for inputs.

interactive_examples.explanation: Evaluates math sum inside double parentheses and outputs result to standard stdout.
interactive_examples.common_mistakes: Using single parentheses which prints string brackets instead.

interactive_examples.objective: Verify if a file exists using test command conditionals.
interactive_examples.explanation: Evaluates if welcome.txt is a regular file. If true (exit code 0), prints string using &&.
interactive_examples.common_mistakes: Forgetting spacing inside brackets (e.g. typing `[-f welcome.txt]` instead of `[ -f welcome.txt ]`).
interactive_examples.tips: Spacing inside bracket tests is strictly required by the bash parser.

lessons.title: 1. Shebang & Setup
lessons.definition: The shebang (#!/bin/bash) on the first line specifies the shell interpreter.
lessons.explanation: Tells the kernel to execute the script using the bash shell runtime environment.
lessons.instruction: Create hello.sh and write shebang '#!/bin/bash' on the first line.

lessons.title: 2. Script Execution
lessons.definition: Scripts are executed either with the 'bash' command or using ./ path notation.
lessons.explanation: Running 'bash script.sh' works even if the file lacks execute permissions.
lessons.instruction: Write echo 'Hello World' inside hello.sh and run it using the bash command.

lessons.title: 3. Shell Variables
lessons.definition: Variables store strings, numbers, or command outputs.
lessons.explanation: Declare with name=val (no spaces!). Access using the dollar sign ($) prefix.
lessons.instruction: Export a custom environment variable named NAME set to student.

lessons.title: 4. Arithmetic Operations
lessons.definition: Double parentheses (( )) evaluate arithmetic expressions in bash.
lessons.explanation: Allows basic calculations like addition, subtraction, multiplication, and divisions.
lessons.instruction: Compute the sum of 10 and 20 using double parentheses and print it.

lessons.title: 5. Numeric Comparisons
lessons.definition: Conditional flags inside [ ] compare numerical values.
lessons.instruction: Test if 15 is greater than 10 using test syntax.

lessons.title: 6. File Check Condition
lessons.definition: The -f conditional checks if a path exists and is a regular file.
lessons.explanation: Often used in scripts to verify configurations exist before reading.
lessons.instruction: Check if welcome.txt exists using the -f test flag.

lessons.title: 7. If Else Conditional
lessons.definition: The if statement runs different code blocks depending on truth evaluation.
lessons.explanation: Syntax: if [ test ]; then commands; else other_commands; fi
lessons.instruction: Run a conditional that creates directory logs if not already existing.
lessons.example: if [ ! -d backups ]; then mkdir backups; fi
lessons.hint: Type: if [ ! -d logs ]; then mkdir logs; fi
lessons.solution: if [ ! -d logs ]; then mkdir logs; fi

lessons.title: 8. Number Loops (For Range)
lessons.definition: For loops iterate a block of code over a list of items.
lessons.explanation: Ranges are represented as {1..5} to repeat actions exactly five times.
lessons.instruction: Iterate numbers 1 to 3, printing each on a new line.
lessons.example: for val in {1..10}; do echo $val; done
lessons.hint: Type: for i in {1..3}; do echo $i; done
lessons.solution: for i in {1..3}; do echo $i; done

lessons.title: 9. File Iterations (For Files)
lessons.definition: For loops can glob wildcards to iterate file sets dynamically.
lessons.explanation: Loops run once for each file matching the pattern (e.g., *.sh).
lessons.instruction: List all .sh files in your directory using a for loop.
lessons.example: for file in *.txt; do echo $file; done
lessons.hint: Type: for f in *.sh; do echo $f; done

lessons.title: 10. While Loop Increment
lessons.definition: While loops execute as long as their condition evaluates to true.
lessons.explanation: Requires a condition that eventually becomes false to prevent infinite loops.
lessons.instruction: Execute a loop printing counter increments while counter is less than 3.
lessons.example: c=1; while [ $c -le 5 ]; do echo $c; c=$((c+1)); done
lessons.hint: Type: c=1; while [ $c -lt 3 ]; do echo $c; c=$((c+1)); done
lessons.solution: c=1; while [ $c -lt 3 ]; do echo $c; c=$((c+1)); done

lessons.title: 11. Positional Arguments
lessons.definition: $1, $2, $3... store arguments passed to the script during launch.
lessons.explanation: These allow script parameterizations. For example, ./run.sh argument1.
lessons.instruction: Print the first argument inside a temporary script call.
lessons.hint: Create script writing 'echo $1' then run: bash arg.sh verified

lessons.title: 12. Interactive Read Input
lessons.definition: The read command pauses script execution to capture keyboard inputs.
lessons.explanation: Prompts user input, storing the response inside a named variable.
lessons.instruction: Read user input into var and echo back. (Use simulation to check commands)

lessons.title: 13. Exit Status Code ($?)
lessons.definition: Every Linux command returns an exit status code between 0 and 255.
lessons.explanation: 0 means success. Any non-zero code represents an error code.
lessons.instruction: Check the exit status of the previous command using echo $?.

lessons.title: 14. Custom Functions
lessons.definition: Functions bundle reusable command steps in shell scripts.
lessons.explanation: Declare using function_name() { ... } structure and call by name.
lessons.instruction: Invoke a function named greet that prints hello.

lessons.title: 15. Local Variable Scope
lessons.definition: The local keyword restricts a variable's visibility to its function.
lessons.explanation: Prevents accidental side effects and name collisions in scripts.
lessons.instruction: Run a function containing a local variable mapping to local_val.
lessons.hint: Type: func() { local val='local_val'; echo $val; }; func

exercises.title: Automation directory backup
exercises.problem: Create a script `backup_run.sh` that checks if directory `data` exists, and if so, copies all contents to a `backup` folder.
exercises.hint: Use `if [ -d data ]; then cp -R data/ backup/; fi`.
exercises.expected_result: Script correctly copies folder data files.

exercises.title: Math increment values
exercises.problem: Write a script that reads numerical value inputs and increments them using a double parentheses construct.

exercises.title: For loop directories listing
exercises.problem: Create a loop iterating through files ending in `.sh` and print their filenames to terminal output.
exercises.hint: Use `for f in *.sh; do echo $f; done`.

exercises.title: Positional inputs configurations
exercises.problem: Construct a deployment script `setup.sh` that checks if the first parameter is 'production' and logs messages.
exercises.expected_result: Prints deployment messages if matching parameter is passed.
exercises.objective: Learn using positional arguments inside conditions.

exercises.title: Custom logs rotator function
exercises.problem: Write a local function `clean_logs()` that empties a temporary file and returns an exit code.
exercises.expected_result: Function runs successfully resetting log content.

exercises.title: While loop delay counter
exercises.problem: Write a loop utility that prints countdown integers from 3 to 1.
exercises.hint: Use `c=3; while [ $c -gt 0 ]; do echo $c; c=$((c - 1)); done`.

quiz.question: Which character combination is known as the shebang and directs the script to the proper shell interpreter?
quiz.explanation: #! points the kernel loader to the program executable parser path (e.g. /bin/bash).

incorrect_explanations./*: Forward slash star represents block comments in languages like C.
incorrect_explanations.$!: Special variable returning PID of last background process.

quiz.question: Which variable declaration is valid in a Bash shell environment?
quiz.explanation: Bash requires name=value syntax with absolutely no spaces around the equal sign.

incorrect_explanations.NAME = student: Fails because the parser interprets 'NAME' as a command and '=' as an argument.
incorrect_explanations.NAME:student: Colon is not the variables assignment operator in bash.
incorrect_explanations.$NAME=student: Dollar prefix reads values, it cannot define new variables assignments.

quiz.question: How do you evaluate arithmetic math calculations inside a script?
quiz.explanation: Double parentheses with dollar symbol prefix are the standard syntax for integer arithmetic expansions.

incorrect_explanations.$[ expr ]: Deprecated syntax for numeric evaluations, no longer recommended.
incorrect_explanations.[ expr ]: Brackets evaluate conditional string/logical tests, not mathematical operations.
incorrect_explanations.expr: Evaluates as a raw text string unless run through backticks.

quiz.question: Which comparison flag is used to check if a numeric value is 'greater than' another?
quiz.explanation: -gt stands for 'greater than' and is used inside logical tests [ ].

quiz.question: What is the role of the -f check flag inside bracket test evaluations?
options: Checks if path is a directory
Checks if path is a regular file
quiz.answer: Checks if path is a regular file
quiz.explanation: -f returns true (exit 0) if the target path points to a valid regular file.

incorrect_explanations.Forces file overrides: -f does not change file access permissions.

quiz.question: How do you correctly close an if conditional block in Bash?
quiz.explanation: fi is 'if' written backward, indicating the end of the conditional logic sequence.

incorrect_explanations.endif: Used in other programming languages or C-Shell, not Bash.
incorrect_explanations.close: Not a keyword terminator in bash programming.

quiz.question: Which Special Variable holds the exit status code of the last executed command?
quiz.explanation: $? holds status (0 for success, non-zero representing system error code).

incorrect_explanations.$#: Holds the count of positional parameters passed to a script.
incorrect_explanations.$@: Represents the array list of all parameters.
incorrect_explanations.$!: Holds the PID of the last background job process.

quiz.question: True or False: Spacing inside bracket logical tests (e.g. `[ $A -gt $B ]`) is completely optional.
quiz.explanation: Brackets are aliases to the test command utility; they strictly require space margins to parse parameters.

quiz.question: What does the local keyword do when declared before variables inside a function?
options: Restricts variable scope strictly to the function
quiz.answer: Restricts variable scope strictly to the function
quiz.explanation: local prevents the variable from leaking out or colliding with global variables in parent scopes.

incorrect_explanations.Makes them system-wide variables: This is done using the export command.
incorrect_explanations.Speeds up mathematical evaluations: Has no effect on calculation runtime speeds.
incorrect_explanations.Locks files permission settings: Does not relate to directory security permissions.

quiz.question: Scenario: You need a script loop that runs exactly 5 times printing counter loops. Which construct is best?
options: for i in {1..5}; do echo $i; done
for i in *.sh; do echo $i; done
quiz.answer: for i in {1..5}; do echo $i; done
quiz.explanation: The range indicator {1..5} runs the block exactly five times, outputting the index each step.

incorrect_explanations.while [ i -eq 5 ]; do echo $i; done: Would trigger syntax errors since i is not initialized, or loop infinitely if i is 5.
incorrect_explanations.if [ $i -lt 5 ]; then echo $i; fi: This is a conditional statement that evaluates once, not a loop structure.
incorrect_explanations.for i in *.sh; do echo $i; done: Loops over files ending in .sh, which depends on folder contents, not a fixed range.

resources.summary: This module covers shebang shell script headers, variables mapping, arithmetic operations, bracket comparisons, conditional blocks, loops, exit status checks, and function wrappers.
resources.cheat_sheet:
#!/bin/bash - shebang line
NAME=val - set variable
$NAME - read variable
$((A+B)) - sum math
[ -f file ] - check file
if [ cond ]; then ...; fi - conditional
for i in list; do ...; done - loop
$? - exit code
func() { ... } - custom function

commands_table.name: read
commands_table.description: Pause execution capturing keyboard standard input values.

commands_table.name: export
commands_table.description: Promotes local variables to environment scope variables.

revision_notes: Spacing inside brackets is strictly mandatory: `[ $x -eq 1 ]`.
Variables set with NAME=val, no spaces around the equal sign.
Use exit status $? directly to check errors after executing system utilities.
beginner_mistakes: Writing script logic without setting files executable permissions (+x), prompting permission denied errors on launch.
Adding spaces around equals during variable declaration: `var = val`.
best_practices: Always declare variables scopes local inside helper shell functions.
Double quote variables representing file paths to prevent space splitting errors.

interview_questions.question: How do you pass arguments to a script?
interview_questions.answer: By passing parameters space-separated after the script command: `./script.sh arg1 arg2`.

interview_questions.question: What is the difference between > and >>?
interview_questions.answer: > overwrites target files contents; >> appends new data lines to the bottom of the files.

additional_practice: Write an automated deployment utility archiving log files into backups folders.
Construct a shell configuration script dynamically writing env variables into a config profile.

books.title: Classic Shell Scripting by Arnold Robbins
books.description: The definitive textbook for automated operations scripts.
